ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for iced gula melaka grass jelly soy milk specifically; closest parent 'soy beverage' (FOODON_03311737) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW (Soya milk) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for the gula melaka and grass jelly variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 'Soy milk' used as closest parent; no specific concept for iced gula melaka grass jelly soy milk exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one cup), comprising soy milk, gula melaka syrup, grass jelly cubes, and ice, as typically served in Singaporean cafes and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Soymilk (soy beverage), unsweetened — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Grass jelly (cincau) — FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; Gula Melaka (palm sugar) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD; composite estimation for served beverage
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO-INFOODS / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for unsweetened soy milk (FDC ID 174832) as the primary base; (2) FAO/INFOODS SEA data for grass jelly (cincau), which is predominantly water with negligible macros; (3) IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for gula melaka (palm sugar) contributing carbohydrate and sugar. Proportions estimated as approximately 70% soy milk, 20% gula melaka syrup, 10% grass jelly by weight in the final beverage. Per-100g values reflect this composite blend.

gi_source.title: Soy milk GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; Grass jelly (cincau) — negligible GI contribution (very low carbohydrate); Gula Melaka (palm sugar) — analogous to coconut sugar GI ~54 per University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Soy milk has a published GI of approximately 34 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Gula melaka (palm sugar) is analogous to coconut sugar with a GI of approximately 54 per University of Sydney GI Database. Grass jelly contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect GI. The composite GI of approximately 44 is estimated by weighting soy milk GI (34) and palm sugar GI (54) by their relative carbohydrate contributions in the beverage. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (44 × 30.6) / 100 ≈ 13.0 g (based on 300 g serving with 10.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~28 mg per 100 g) and potassium presence from soy milk
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 84 mg sodium per 300 g serving, this beverage is low in sodium and suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though the gula melaka syrup adds calories and should be accounted for in overall dietary planning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added palm sugar (gula melaka) contributes significant free sugars (~25.5 g per serving) and a moderate glycemic load (~13)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this beverage with caution due to the gula melaka content, which raises blood glucose; requesting reduced or no added sugar and monitoring portion size is advisable to limit glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based protein from soy, and absence of cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The soy milk base provides isoflavones and plant protein with minimal saturated fat and zero cholesterol, which is broadly consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern, though the high added sugar from gula melaka should be moderated for optimal cardiovascular benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Gula Melaka Grass Jelly Soy Milk is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian cold beverage combining three key components: unsweetened or lightly sweetened soy milk, gula melaka (a rich, aromatic palm sugar syrup derived from the sap of the Arenga pinnata palm), and grass jelly (cincau hitam), which are firm, dark, slightly bitter cubes made from the dried and processed Platostoma palustre herb. The drink is served over ice, creating a refreshing contrast of sweet, earthy, and subtly bitter flavours with a creamy, smooth soy milk base and a soft, silky jelly texture. Gula melaka imparts a distinctive caramel-coconut sweetness that differs markedly from refined white sugar, and is a hallmark ingredient in Southeast Asian desserts and beverages. Nutritionally, the beverage provides plant-based protein (~7.2 g per 300 g serving) and isoflavones from soy milk, with moderate carbohydrate content driven primarily by the palm sugar. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 44 (low GI), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~13) due to the volume of added sugar. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake due to free sugar content from gula melaka. The beverage is dairy-free, vegan, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, making it broadly accessible. It is commonly found in Singaporean hawker centres, cafes, and food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite beverage as served commercially. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data drawn from USDA FoodData Central (soy milk), FAO/INFOODS SEA (grass jelly), and IMR Malaysia MyFCD (gula melaka). GI is an analogous estimate weighted by carbohydrate contribution. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from input 'Iced Gula Melaka Grass Jelly Soy Mllk' (typographic error 'Mllk') to canonical 'Iced Gula Melaka Grass Jelly Soy Milk'. 2. Food group reclassified from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Beverages', which is the correct category for a cold drink. 3. Cuisine reclassified from 'Global' to 'Singaporean' as this is a well-established Singaporean/Malaysian beverage; multilingual naming applied accordingly. 4. Cuisine tags from input ('Ramen', 'Pizza', 'Pasta') are irrelevant to this food and have been discarded. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as soy milk and plant-based ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all ingredients are plant-derived. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is an unfortified plant-based beverage; no animal-derived ingredients present. 8. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources; soy milk is not assumed to be fortified in this hawker/cafe context. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or IMR MyFCD component data used. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 12. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate: soy milk GI ~34 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) weighted with gula melaka/palm sugar GI ~54 (University of Sydney GI Database, coconut sugar analogue) by carbohydrate contribution; gi_category is 'low' (GI ≤ 55). 13. Glycemic load per serving = (44 × 30.6 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 13.0. 14. Restaurant source 'Flavours by Saute - Funan' preserved from input for provenance traceability but not used as a nutritional evidence source.
